channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: What’s in Trump’s New Counterterrorism Strategy?
item.description: The White House ignores the threat of far-right groups.

item.title: How the Abraham Accords Fueled a New Era of Conflict
item.description: Despite promising peace, Israeli-Gulf cooperation paved the path to war with Iran.

item.title: Starmer Braces for Upsets in U.K. Local Elections
item.description: British voters are expected to abandon the Labour Party for nonmainstream alternatives.

item.title: Who Is Xi’s Real No. 2?
item.description: The Chinese leader isn’t willing to give anybody else power.

item.title: How Russia planned to help Iran kill Americans
item.description: It proposed sending unjammable fibre-optic drones to use against American forces

item.title: Media Capture Failed in Hungary-and America Could Be Next
item.description: Former Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán spent 16 years perfecting a model of media capture that aspiring autocrats around the world now seek to emulate. But his defeat showed that even vast propaganda machines are neither impermeable nor infallible in the face of a determined independent press.

item.title: China’s Former Defense Ministers Sentenced to Death With Reprieve: The Reason and the Wider Implications
item.description: Until now, no one from the military top brass had received such severe punishments since early days of the anti-corruption campaign.

item.title: The Blind Spots of African Development Finance
item.description: Africa’s development-finance system is expanding rapidly, with new instruments and investors entering markets across the continent. Yet the analytical capacity needed to track risks and price them in real time has not kept pace, allowing risks to build.

item.title: Telling Turan’s Tales With Agnieszka Pikulicka
item.description: For a region little covered in the global press, every thoughtful effort to tell local stories is a bright light in the dark.

item.title: India’s 2026 State Polls Power up the Modi Juggernaut, Reshape the Opposition
item.description: The opposition has raised concerns over possible electoral manipulations, threats to the federal structure, and democratic balance.

item.title: India’s New Globalization Raj
item.description: India’s deliberate, strategic shift from economic seclusion to global engagement is arguably one of the most consequential structural changes of the 21st century. Indian leaders now understand that self-reliance is achieved through integration, not autarky, and the results speak for themselves.

item.title: What to Make of Adilet, Kazakhstan’s Newest Political Party?
item.description: With Aibek Dadebay – until very recently the head of the presidential administration – at its head, Adilet looks to be just another pro-presidential parrot.

item.title: North Korea’s Constitutional Amendments Signal a Policy of Assurance Toward South Korea
item.description: North Korea wants to assure the South – and Pyongyang’s Russian and Chinese allies – of its intention not to attack Seoul first.

item.title: Why Japan and South Korea Won’t Go Nuclear
item.description: America still needs to worry about proliferation in East Asia.

item.title: The world must stop AI from empowering bioterrorists
item.description: The threat from new pathogens is an even graver danger than AI-backed hackers

item.title: The Trump-Xi summit will expose a dysfunctional duo
item.description: Mutual vulnerability is no substitute for global leadership

item.title: Artificial intelligence revives a cold-war-style dilemma
item.description: America and China are weighing co-operation and competition

item.title: A Chinese high-seas misadventure in luxury yachts
item.description: A state-owned enterprise may have to walk the plank

item.title: American subs rule beneath the waves, but China’s are catching up
item.description: And underwater drones may supplant them all

item.title: The Supreme Court has become a great place to build your brand
item.description: On the left and the right, justices are becoming more preoccupied with their own ideas than the court’s institutional legitimacy

item.title: Foreign businesses: have you considered America?
item.description: A report from the basement of the SelectUSA Investment Summit

item.title: The Democratic approach to AI is not all about bans
item.description: Rahm Emanuel charts a centrist path for AI

item.title: The Supreme Court has unleashed the gerrymanderers
item.description: Louisiana v Callais is baleful for democracy

item.title: Trump’s threat to withdraw soldiers is more serious than it seems
item.description: The unit affected offers a vital capability

item.title: Toxic smoke and fire flowing like lava
item.description: War has caused one of Russia’s worst environmental disasters at Tuapse

item.title: Why Swedish schools are going unplugged
item.description: Nordic pupils are returning to books and pens

item.title: Can a beauty mega-deal save Estée Lauder?
item.description: America’s cosmetics champion wants to buy its way back to better days

item.title: The pros and cons of commuting
item.description: Everyone moans about the length of their commute. Should managers care?

item.title: Airlines are grappling with dwindling supplies of jet fuel
item.description: Yet it is unclear where shortages will hit first and hardest

item.title: Mali shows the growing strength of jihadism in the Sahel
item.description: There is little to stop militants from expanding their influence

item.title: Claudia Sheinbaum is in a bind, with her party accused of corruption
item.description: How she deals with America’s indictment of Morena officials could define her presidency

item.title: Venezuela’s 100-year territorial dispute is back in court
item.description: The regime claims the Essequibo region of Guyana, and its oil

item.title: China is pushing Donald Trump for concessions on Taiwan
item.description: Some in Taipei and Washington fear he may give ground

item.title: Trump and Xi will struggle to strike a major economic deal
item.description: Just avoiding a renewed clash will count as success

item.title: Business
The weekly cartoon
Politics
Foreign firms: have you considered America?
item.description: A report from the basement of the SelectUSA Investment Summit

item.title: Trump’s threat to withdraw troops is serious for Europe
item.description: The unit affected offers a vital capability

item.title: A Ukrainian strike on a Russian oil hub causes catastrophe
item.description: Toxic smoke billowed and molten fuel flowed like lava

item.title: China’s Unwinding of the Manus Deal Highlights a Key US Advantage
item.description: Who wants to build a business in a place where you can get rich but you can never leave?

item.title: The UAE’s OPEC Exit: Why It Happened and What It Means for Asia
item.description: The UAE is charting its own path. That matters for Asia.

item.title: How the Hang Seng Is Thriving as Geopolitical Uncertainty Impacts Global Markets
item.description: Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index is largely unphased by the global energy crisis.

item.title: Debtor Countries Finally Have a Group of Their Own
item.description: As systemic and geopolitical risks mount, financial uncertainty has become the new normal for developing countries, leading to a growing chorus of Global South policymakers calling for more collaboration and coordination. The newly formed Borrowers’ Platform makes this a reality.

item.title: The gutting of USAID has left a void China will not fill
item.description: China lends $35 for every dollar it donates. That is not a substitute for American aid

item.title: Why the Upcoming China-US Summit Is Likely to Be Misread
item.description: The return to high-level engagement after a period of escalation reflects adaptation, not forthcoming concessions.

item.title: Diplomacy or more war? Iran’s leaders are split
item.description: Some are convinced that talking is futile

item.title: Arab rulers have little sympathy for Iran
item.description: Their people are not always so hostile

item.title: Narendra Modi’s party is on a roll in India
item.description: The BJP has beaten a bad ruler in West Bengal. But India must not become a de facto one-party state

item.title: How China Is Winning the Global AI Race
item.description: Cutting-edge U.S. models are too expensive for much of the world.

item.title: The energy shock triggers an Asian dash for biofuels
item.description: But tackling one crisis could exacerbate another

item.title: Can Diplomacy Alone Secure India’s Critical Minerals Future?
item.description: India has recognized that critical minerals are a central pillar of its energy security, industrial strategy and geopolitical positioning.

item.title: The Iran War’s Strategic Fallout
item.description: By leaving US allies and rivals alike scrambling to respond to an unpredictable and unreliable superpower, the war in the Middle East is driving an historic geopolitical realignment that will shift the global balance of power over the next decade. The scale of the changes will rival those following the end of the Cold War.

item.title: The myth of the petrodollar
item.description: The dominance of America’s currency runs deeper than oil

item.title: Vladimir Putin Is Much Weaker Than You Think
item.description: Used to outwitting his enemies, the Russian leader is running out of room for maneuver.

item.title: DeepSeek and Alibaba rescue China’s office landlords
item.description: Technology firms are reviving (a few) Chinese commercial-property markets

item.title: Unicredit’s lowball bid for Commerzbank causes consternation
item.description: A bad-tempered battle for a big German bank

item.title: Europe is unshackling business. But not enough
item.description: Why market liberals must win the battle for Brussels—and national capitals, too

item.title: Donald Trump’s foreign policy gets a muscular finance arm
item.description: The Development Finance Corporation’s loan book may soon rival the World Bank’s

item.title: Reasserting Public Scrutiny Over Indonesia’s Foreign Policy
item.description: At a time when Jakarta's foreign policy decisions are particularly consequential and sometimes controversial, public scrutiny of them has strangely diminished.

item.title: Malaysia’s Hotline to Tehran
item.description: When Malaysian oil tankers became stranded in the Persian Gulf, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim phoned a friend.

item.title: Why the Rupiah is Weakening
item.description: Despite what the alarming drop in the value of its currency might suggest, Indonesia is not on the brink of an economic crisis.

item.title: Thailand Unilaterally Voids Maritime Boundary Agreement With Cambodia
item.description: The 2001 MoU set up a framework for discussions on joint oil and gas exploration in areas of the Gulf of Thailand claimed by both sides.

item.title: War Handed Public Space Back to Iran’s Regime
item.description: After years of quiet encroachment by ordinary people, the Islamic Republic reclaims the urban landscape.

item.title: This Is Not the World Russia Wants
item.description: A belligerent America is foiling Putin’s strategy.

item.title: Philippines Urges Myanmar to Grant ASEAN Special Envoy Access to Aung San Suu Kyi
item.description: The request came a day ahead of the 48th ASEAN Summit in Cebu, where the Myanmar conflict will once again be high on the agenda.

item.title: Despite Donald Trump’s talk, a lasting peace is some way off
item.description: Iran is now targeting the UAE

item.title: Navigating the Many Issues Surrounding China’s Ports Abroad
item.description: An array of economic, political, and security issues are associated with China’s overseas ports.

item.title: A Year After the War: Kashmir’s Border Civilians Sit Between Shelled Roofs and Self-Made Bunkers
item.description: “We can rely on bunkers, but not on ceasefires.”
